title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who write about travel. For questions 43-52, choose from the people (A-D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Writing the World: Four People on Travel Writing

texts.A:
### Beatrice, 29, Travel Blogger
[45]Unlike most travel writers who focus on luxury or adventure, I deliberately seek out places that are considered too ordinary or overlooked to attract tourism - small industrial towns, unfashionable suburbs, unremarkable coastlines - and I try to find what is genuinely interesting about them.[/45] My readership is loyal and engaged because they are tired of aspirational content. [47]The commercial side is harder than it looks: brand partnerships are the main source of income but they require maintaining credibility at the same time, and that balance takes constant negotiation.[/47] [52]I started travelling alone at eighteen and the combination of freedom and uncertainty shaped how I observe and question everything around me - I genuinely do not think I would be the writer I am without those early solo years.[/52]
texts.B:
### Noel, 54, Travel Magazine Editor
[43]The explosion of travel content online has paradoxically made it harder to sustain a print magazine that distinguishes itself - when every destination has been photographed from every angle and written about a thousand times, originality becomes extraordinarily difficult.[/43] We have had to rethink what print offers that digital cannot. [48]The travel writing I admire most reads like fiction: it has character, tension, a narrative arc, and a specific voice that makes you feel you are alongside the writer rather than simply receiving information.[/48] Travel writing at its best is literature, and it has to be held to that standard.
texts.C:
### Zara, 37, Freelance Journalist and Author
Travelling widely for twenty years has changed my perspective on nearly everything. [44]More than anything, spending extended time in other countries made me realise how many of my assumptions about my home country were simply cultural habits I had never examined - travel made me a sharper, more curious observer of my own society.[/44] [49]But I want to be honest about what travel writing actually involves: weeks of budget accommodation, working late into the night on deadlines, long-haul travel on minimal sleep, and a great deal of rejection before any of it is published.[/49] It is not the romantic profession it appears. [51]I also feel strongly that travel writers have a responsibility not to reduce places and people to attractive backdrops for their own narratives - the communities we describe are not props.[/51]
texts.D:
### Hamid, 43, Documentary Filmmaker
[46]Wherever I work, I operate by a firm personal rule: I spend at least one full week in a location before I start filming anything, because I believe you cannot honestly represent a place if you have not first listened to it.[/46] The temptation to rush straight to the picturesque shot is one every documentary maker has to resist. [50]Making a documentary about a place demands a form of patience that is quite different from written journalism - you are waiting for real moments rather than constructing them, and that requires complete attention to what is happening around you.[/50] The camera teaches you to see differently.

questions.question: describes how the growth of digital media has created a challenge for their area of the industry?
questions.explanation: Noel says the explosion of travel content online has made it harder to sustain a print magazine because originality becomes extraordinarily difficult.

questions.question: explains how travel gave them a new perspective on their own country?
questions.explanation: Zara says spending extended time in other countries made her realise how many of her assumptions about home were cultural habits she had never examined.

questions.question: takes a deliberately different approach to choosing what to write about compared to other travel writers?
questions.explanation: Beatrice seeks out ordinary, overlooked places rather than the luxury or adventure destinations that most travel writers focus on.

questions.question: describes a personal rule they follow before starting work in a new location?
questions.explanation: Hamid always spends at least one full week in a location before filming because he believes you cannot honestly represent a place unless you have listened to it.

questions.question: explains the financial reality of their work as a travel content creator?
questions.explanation: Beatrice says brand partnerships are her main income source but require maintaining credibility, a balance that takes constant negotiation.

questions.question: argues that travel writing should meet the standards of a different literary form?
questions.explanation: Noel says the travel writing he admires reads like fiction, with character, tension, and a specific voice, and argues it should be held to that standard.

questions.question: challenges the popular image of travel writing as a glamorous occupation?
questions.explanation: Zara says travel writing involves budget accommodation, late-night deadlines, minimal sleep and a great deal of rejection - not the romantic profession it appears.

questions.question: describes how their particular medium requires a different kind of focus or attention?
questions.explanation: Hamid says making a documentary demands patience different from written journalism - waiting for real moments rather than constructing them.

questions.question: expresses concern about the ethical responsibility of travel writers towards the places they describe?
questions.explanation: Zara feels strongly that travel writers must not reduce places and people to attractive backdrops for their own narratives.

questions.question: credits their earliest travel experiences with shaping them as a writer?
questions.explanation: Beatrice says she started travelling alone at eighteen and that the combination of freedom and uncertainty shaped how she observes and questions everything.
